Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) Labor Supply Literature Review
runs.reasoningSummary: Empirical estimates of the income elasticity of labor supply (the income effect) are consistently negative but typically small in magnitude. In microsimulation contexts (like those examining EITC or tax reforms), researchers often calibrate this parameter to values between -0.05 and -0.15, as large negative values would imply unrealistic reductions in labor effort as income rises, while positive values are rarely supported by data for primary earners.
